str:Code.com:Description: Expenditure refers to the total outflow of resources, including payments, purchases, and transfers, made by a government entity during a specific period to fulfill its various functions and obligations. It encompasses spending on goods and services, wages and salaries, social benefits, debt servicing, infrastructure investments, and other government programs and activities. Expenditure represents the utilization of government funds to provide public services, maintain infrastructure, implement policies, and meet financial obligations. Monitoring expenditure patterns and allocations provides insight into government priorities, policy objectives, and fiscal sustainability.

str:Code.com:Name: Social benefits in cash
str:Code.com:Description: Cash payments provided by the government to individuals or households as social benefits.

str:Code.com:Name: Social benefits in kind
str:Code.com:Description: Non-cash benefits provided by the government to individuals or households as social benefits.

str:Code.com:Name: Social transfers in kind (purchased market production)
str:Code.com:Description: Non-cash benefits provided by the government through the purchase of goods and services from the market, distributed to beneficiaries.

str:Code.com:Name: Total expenditure and net lending / net borrowing
str:Code.com:Description: Expenditure refers to the total outflow of resources, including payments, purchases, and transfers, made by a government entity during a specific period to fulfill its various functions and obligations. It encompasses spending on goods and services, wages and salaries, social benefits, debt servicing, infrastructure investments, and other government programs and activities. Expenditure represents the utilization of government funds to provide public services, maintain infrastructure, implement policies, and meet financial obligations. Monitoring expenditure patterns and allocations provides insight into government priorities, policy objectives, and fiscal sustainability. Net lending is defined as the difference between changes in net worth due to saving and capital transfers and net acquisitions of non-financial assets (acquisitions less disposals of non-financial assets, less depreciation). If the amount is negative, it represents net borrowing. In the external accounts, it represents the sum of the balances on the current and capital accounts. This is conceptually equal to the net balance of the financial account. If the balance is positive, it is called net lending, implying that in net terms, the economy supplies funds to the rest of the world. If the balance is negative, it is called net borrowing, implying that in net terms, the economy receives funds from the rest of the world.

str:Code.com:Name: Primary expenditure (expenditure less interest payable)
str:Code.com:Description: The government's total expenditure excluding interest payments on debt.

str:Code.com:Name: Net transactions in assets and liabilities
str:Code.com:Description: The difference between the acquisition and disposal of assets and the incurrence and repayment of liabilities by the government.

str:Code.com:Name: Investment in nonfinancial assets (acquisitions less disposals)
str:Code.com:Description: Expenditures by the government on acquiring, constructing, or improving physical assets such as infrastructure, buildings, and equipment.

str:Code.com:Name: Acquisitions of nonfinancial assets: domestic
str:Code.com:Description: The purchase or acquisition of nonfinancial assets within the domestic economy. These assets include physical assets like infrastructure, buildings, machinery, and land.

str:Code.com:Name: Acquisitions of nonfinancial assets
str:Code.com:Description: Assets other than financial assets, where financial assets repressent financial claims, and gold bullion held by monetary authorities as a reserve asset.

str:Code.com:Name: Acquisitions of nonfinancial assets: excluding cash
str:Code.com:Description: The purchase or acquisition of nonfinancial assets excluding cash transactions. This includes physical assets and other non-monetary items.

str:Code.com:Name: Disposals of nonfinancial assets
str:Code.com:Description: The process of selling, transferring, or otherwise disposing of nonfinancial assets, such as land, buildings, and equipment.

str:Code.com:Name: Consumption of fixed capital
str:Code.com:Description: The decline in the value of fixed assets over time due to wear and tear, obsolescence, or other factors. This concept is similar to depreciation in business accounting.

str:Code.com:Name: Net acquisition of financial assets
str:Code.com:Description: The difference between the acquisition and disposal of financial assets by the government, representing the net increase in financial assets.

str:Code.com:Name: Net acquisition of financial assets, policy lending
str:Code.com:Description: The net increase in the government's financial assets resulting from policy-driven lending activities, such as loans to support economic development.

str:Code.com:Name: Acquisitions of nonfinancial assets: foreign
str:Code.com:Description: The purchase or acquisition of nonfinancial assets located in foreign economies. These can include properties, businesses, and other physical assets outside the domestic economy.

str:Code.com:Name: Net acquisition of financial assets (adjustment)
str:Code.com:Description: Adjustments made to the recorded net acquisition of financial assets to account for corrections, reallocations, or other changes.

str:Code.com:Name: Net acquisition of financial assets other than cash
str:Code.com:Description: The net increase in the government's financial assets excluding cash, such as investments in securities or loans.

str:Code.com:Name: Net acquisition of financial assets other than cash: domestic
str:Code.com:Description: The net increase in the government's financial assets within the domestic economy, excluding cash.

str:Code.com:Name: Net acquisition of financial assets other than cash: foreign
str:Code.com:Description: The net increase in the government's financial assets in foreign economies, excluding cash.

str:Code.com:Name: Net incurrence of liabilities
str:Code.com:Description: The net increase in the government's liabilities to domestic creditors, representing new borrowing from domestic sources minus repayments.

str:Code.com:Name: Net incurrence of liabilities: domestic
str:Code.com:Description: The net increase in the government's liabilities within the domestic economy, representing new borrowing minus repayments.

str:Code.com:Name: Net incurrence of liabilities: foreign
str:Code.com:Description: The net increase in the government's liabilities in foreign economies, representing new borrowing minus repayments.

str:Code.com:Name: Net incurrence of liabilities (adjustment)
str:Code.com:Description: Adjustments made to the recorded net incurrence of liabilities to account for corrections, reallocations, or other changes.

str:Code.com:Name: Net incurrence of foreign liabilities (adjustment)
str:Code.com:Description: Adjustments made to the recorded net incurrence of foreign liabilities to account for corrections, reallocations, or other changes.

str:Code.com:Name: Change in net debt due to transactions
str:Code.com:Description: The change in the government's net debt resulting from economic transactions, including borrowing, repayments, and other financial activities.

str:Code.com:Name: Net incurrence of liabilities: domestic debtors
str:Code.com:Description: The net increase in the government's liabilities to domestic debtors, representing new borrowing from domestic sources minus repayments.

str:Code.com:Name: Net incurrence of liabilities: external creditors
str:Code.com:Description: The net increase in the government's liabilities to foreign creditors, representing new borrowing from foreign sources minus repayments.

str:Code.com:Name: Net incurrence of liabilities: external debtors
str:Code.com:Description: The net increase in the government's liabilities to foreign debtors, representing new borrowing from foreign sources minus repayments.

str:Code.com:Name: Own-account capital formation
str:Code.com:Description: The process of creating capital assets by the government for its own use rather than purchasing them.

str:Code.com:Name: Own-account capital formation: compensation of employees
str:Code.com:Description: The cost of employee compensation involved in the creation of capital assets for the government's own use.

str:Code.com:Name: Own-account capital formation: use of goods and services
str:Code.com:Description: The cost of goods and services consumed in the creation of capital assets for the government's own use.

str:Code.com:Name: Own-account capital formation: consumption of fixed capital
str:Code.com:Description: The depreciation of fixed assets involved in the creation of capital assets for the government's own use.

str:Code.com:Name: Own-account capital formation: other taxes on producation minus other subsidies on production
str:Code.com:Description: The net amount of other taxes on production minus subsidies on production involved in the creation of capital assets for the government's own use.

str:Code.com:Name: Net transactions in financial assets and liabilities
str:Code.com:Description: The difference between the acquisition and disposal of financial assets and the incurrence and repayment of financial liabilities by the government.

str:Code.com:Name: Change in net worth due to holding gains and losses
str:Code.com:Description: The change in the government's net worth resulting from changes in the value of its assets and liabilities due to market fluctuations or other factors.

str:Code.com:Name: Holding gains and losses in nonfinancial assets
str:Code.com:Description: Changes in the value of nonfinancial assets due to market fluctuations or other factors.

str:Code.com:Name: Holding gains and losses in financial assets
str:Code.com:Description: Changes in the value of financial assets due to market fluctuations or other factors.

str:Code.com:Name: Holding gains and losses in liabilities
str:Code.com:Description: Changes in the value of liabilities due to market fluctuations or other factors.

str:Code.com:Name: Change in net debt due to holding gains and losses
str:Code.com:Description: The change in the government's net debt resulting from changes in the value of its debt instruments due to market fluctuations or other factors.

str:Code.com:Name: Change in net financial worth due to holding gains and losses
str:Code.com:Description: The change in the government's net financial worth resulting from changes in the value of its financial assets and liabilities due to market fluctuations or other factors.

str:Code.com:Name: Change in net worth due to volume changes
str:Code.com:Description: The change in the government's net worth due to changes in the volume of assets and liabilities.

str:Code.com:Name: Other volume changes in nonfinancial assets
str:Code.com:Description: Changes in the value of nonfinancial assets due to factors other than transactions, such as revaluations or other volume changes.

str:Code.com:Name: Other volume changes in financial assets
str:Code.com:Description: Changes in the value of financial assets due to factors other than transactions, such as revaluations or other volume changes.

str:Code.com:Name: Other volume changes in liabilities
str:Code.com:Description: Changes in the value of liabilities due to factors other than transactions, such as revaluations or other volume changes.

str:Code.com:Name: Change in net debt due to volume changes
str:Code.com:Description: The change in the government's net debt due to changes in the volume of debt instruments, such as issuance or redemption of bonds.

str:Code.com:Name: Change in net financial worth due to volume changes
str:Code.com:Description: The change in the government's net financial worth due to changes in the volume of financial assets and liabilities.

str:Code.com:Name: Net worth
str:Code.com:Description: The difference between the government's total assets and total liabilities, representing its net financial position.

str:Code.com:Name: Net worth lagged by one period
str:Code.com:Description: The government's net worth from the previous accounting period, used for comparative analysis.

str:Code.com:Name: Closing stock position of nonfinancial assets
str:Code.com:Description: The value of the government's nonfinancial assets at the end of a specific period.

str:Code.com:Name: Closing stock position of financial assets
str:Code.com:Description: The value of the government's financial assets at the end of a specific period.

str:Code.com:Name: Closing stock position of financial assets: domestic
str:Code.com:Description: The value of the government's financial assets held within the domestic economy at the end of a specific period.

str:Code.com:Name: Closing stock position of financial assets: foreign
str:Code.com:Description: The value of the government's financial assets held in foreign economies at the end of a specific period.

str:Code.com:Name: Closing stock position of liabilities
str:Code.com:Description: The value of the government's liabilities at the end of a specific period.

str:Code.com:Name: Closing stock position of liabilities: domestic
str:Code.com:Description: The value of the government's liabilities held within the domestic economy at the end of a specific period.

str:Code.com:Name: Closing stock position of liabilities: foreign
str:Code.com:Description: The value of the government's liabilities held in foreign economies at the end of a specific period.

str:Code.com:Name: Closing stock position of contingent liabilities
str:Code.com:Description: The value of contingent liabilities that the government holds at the end of a specific period. Contingent liabilities are potential obligations that may arise based on the outcome of future events.

str:Code.com:Name: Gross debt
str:Code.com:Description: Gross debt is the sum of all liabilities that are debt instruments. Debt instruments are financial instruments that require the payment of principal and/or interest at some point(s) in the future. Debt instruments consist of SDRs, currency and deposits, debt securities, loans, insurance technical reserves, pension and related entitlements, provision for calls under standardized guarantees, and other accounts receivable/payable.

str:Code.com:Name: Gross debt denominated in domestic currency
str:Code.com:Description: The total amount of government debt denominated in the domestic currency.

str:Code.com:Name: Gross debt denominated in foreign currency
str:Code.com:Description: The total amount of government debt denominated in foreign currencies.

str:Code.com:Name: Net debt
str:Code.com:Description: Net debt is the sum of all liabilities less financial assets that are debt instruments. Debt instruments are financial instruments that require the payment of principal and/or interest at some point(s) in the future. Debt instruments consist of SDRs, currency and deposits, debt securities, loans, insurance technical reserves, pension and related entitlements, provision for calls under standardized guarantees, and other accounts receivable/payable.

str:Code.com:Name: Closing stock position of liabilities net of currency and deposits assets
str:Code.com:Description: The value of the government's liabilities minus its assets held in the form of currency and deposits at the end of a specific period.

str:Code.com:Name: Closing net financial worth
str:Code.com:Description: The government's net financial worth at the end of a specific period, calculated as the difference between its financial assets and liabilities.

str:Code.com:Name: Implicit transfers resulting from loan liabilities at concessional interest rates
str:Code.com:Description: Financial transfers that result from loans provided at below-market interest rates, representing a form of subsidy.

str:Code.com:Name: Change in net worth due to other economic flows
str:Code.com:Description: The change in the government's net worth due to factors other than transactions, such as revaluations or other changes in volume.

str:Code.com:Name: Other economic flows in nonfinancial assets
str:Code.com:Description: Changes in the value of nonfinancial

str:Code.com:Name: Other economic flows in financial assets
str:Code.com:Description: Changes in the value of financial assets due to economic flows other than transactions, such as revaluations or other volume changes.

str:Code.com:Name: Other economic flows in liabilities
str:Code.com:Description: Changes in the value of liabilities due to economic flows other than transactions, such as revaluations or other volume changes.

str:Code.com:Name: Change in net debt due to other economic flows
str:Code.com:Description: The change in the government's net debt due to factors other than transactions, such as revaluations or other changes in volume.

str:Code.com:Name: Change in net financial worth due to other economic flows
str:Code.com:Description: The change in the government's net financial worth due to factors other than transactions, such as revaluations or other changes in volume.

str:Code.com:Name: Purchases of nonfinancial assets
str:Code.com:Description: Expenditures by the government on acquiring physical assets such as infrastructure, buildings, and equipment.

str:Code.com:Name: Gross financing requirement
str:Code.com:Description: The total amount of financing needed to cover the government's budget deficit and refinance maturing debt. In general this reflects the general government fiscal deficit (excluding grants) and general government external and domestic amortization

str:Code.com:Name: Government balance per national definition
str:Code.com:Description: The government's fiscal balance as defined by national accounting standards, reflecting the difference between total revenue and total expenditure.

str:Code.com:Name: Gross operating balance
str:Code.com:Description: The difference between total revenue and total operating expenses, excluding capital expenditures and interest payments.

str:Code.com:Name: Cash inflow from financing activities
str:Code.com:Description: The cash received from financing activities such as borrowing, issuing bonds, or receiving grants.

str:Code.com:Name: Net lending (+) / net borrowing (-)
str:Code.com:Description: Net lending is defined as the difference between changes in net worth due to saving and capital transfers and net acquisitions of non-financial assets (acquisitions less disposals of non-financial assets, less depreciation). If the amount is negative, it represents net borrowing. In the external accounts, it represents the sum of the balances on the current and capital accounts. This is conceptually equal to the net balance of the financial account. If the balance is positive, it is called net lending, implying that in net terms, the economy supplies funds to the rest of the world. If the balance is negative, it is called net borrowing, implying that in net terms, the economy receives funds from the rest of the world.

str:Code.com:Name: Net lending (+) / net borrowing (-) (adjustment)
str:Code.com:Description: Adjustments made to the recorded net lending or borrowing to account for corrections, reallocations, or other changes.

str:Code.com:Name: Net lending (+) / net borrowing (-) excluding social security schemes
str:Code.com:Description: The government's net lending or borrowing excluding the transactions and balances related to social security schemes.

str:Code.com:Name: Net operating balance
str:Code.com:Description: The difference between the government's total revenue and total operating expenses, excluding capital expenditures and interest payments.

str:Code.com:Name: Net operating balance (adjustment)
str:Code.com:Description: Adjustments made to the recorded net operating balance to account for corrections, reallocations, or other changes.

str:Code.com:Name: Primary net lending (+) / net borrowing (-)
str:Code.com:Description: Primary net lending/borrowing refers to the difference between a government's total revenue (excluding borrowing) and its total expenditures (excluding debt repayments and interest payments). It represents the government's fiscal position, indicating whether it is running a surplus (positive value) or a deficit (negative value) before considering the impact of debt servicing costs.

str:Code.com:Name: Primary net cash inflow from operating activities
str:Code.com:Description: The net amount of cash generated from the government's operating activities excluding interest payments.

str:Code.com:Name: Intermediate consumption
str:Code.com:Description: The value of goods and services consumed as inputs in the production process, excluding fixed assets. This consumption is subtracted from the total output to calculate value added.

str:Code.com:Name: Indirect tax
str:Code.com:Description: An?indirect tax?(such as sales tax, per unit tax, value-added tax (VAT), or goods and services tax (GST), excise, consumption tax, tariff) is a?tax?that is levied upon goods and services before they reach the customer who ultimately pays the indirect tax as a part of market price of the good or service purchased.

str:Code.com:Name: Memo item: expenditure
str:Code.com:Description: An additional item in financial accounts that records expenditure not included in the main accounting categories, used for supplementary information.

str:Code.com:Name: Market output
str:Code.com:Description: The total value of goods and services produced for sale at market prices.

str:Code.com:Name: Net acquisition of nonfinancial assets
str:Code.com:Description: The difference between the acquisition and disposal of nonfinancial assets by the government, representing the net increase in physical assets.

str:Code.com:Name: Net acquisition of nonfinancial assets (adjustment)
str:Code.com:Description: Adjustments made to the recorded net acquisition of nonfinancial assets to account for corrections, reallocations, or other changes.

str:Code.com:Name: Net change in the stock of cash
str:Code.com:Description: The difference between the opening and closing balances of cash held by the government over a specific period.

str:Code.com:Name: Net change in the stock of financial assets/liabilities
str:Code.com:Description: The difference between the opening and closing balances of financial assets and liabilities held by the government over a specific period.

str:Code.com:Name: Net cash inflow from financing activities
str:Code.com:Description: The net amount of cash received from financing activities, including borrowing and issuing bonds, minus repayments and interest payments.

str:Code.com:Name: Net financial worth
str:Code.com:Description: The difference between the government's total financial assets and total liabilities, representing its net financial position.

str:Code.com:Name: Stock-flow discrepancy, net financial worth
str:Code.com:Description: The difference between changes in stocks (assets and liabilities) and flows (transactions) that affect the net financial worth of the government.

str:Code.com:Name: Net nonlife insurance premiums
str:Code.com:Description: The total amount of premiums received from nonlife insurance policies, minus claims and administrative costs.

str:Code.com:Name: Non-tax revenues
str:Code.com:Description: Government revenue generated from sources other than taxes, such as fees, fines, and income from state-owned enterprises.

str:Code.com:Name: Other non-tax revenues
str:Code.com:Description: Revenue not classified elsewhere in taxes, social contributions, grants receivable, or non-tax revenue from extractive industries.

str:Code.com:Name: Non-tax revenue from extractive industries
str:Code.com:Description: Revenue generated from non-tax sources in the extractive industries, such as royalties, fees, and sales of extracted resources.

str:Code.com:Name: Other non-tax revenue
str:Code.com:Description: Revenue not classified elsewhere in taxes, social contributions, grants receivable, or non-tax revenue from extractive industries.

str:Code.com:Name: Net privatization proceeds
str:Code.com:Description: The net amount of revenue received from the sale of government-owned assets or enterprises, after deducting the costs associated with the privatization process.

str:Code.com:Name: Other changes in the volume of assets
str:Code.com:Description: Changes in the value of assets due to factors other than transactions, such as revaluations or other volume changes.

str:Code.com:Name: Overall fiscal balance
str:Code.com:Description: The difference between the government's total revenue and total expenditure, indicating whether it is running a surplus or deficit.

str:Code.com:Name: Other revenue: interest income
str:Code.com:Description: Interest income earned by the government from various sources, excluding taxes.

str:Code.com:Name: Other taxes on production less other subsidies on production
str:Code.com:Description: The net amount of taxes on production minus subsidies on production that do not fit into specific categories.

str:Code.com:Name: Overall balance
str:Code.com:Description: The overall balance represents the difference between a government's total revenue and its total expenditure over a specific period, typically a fiscal year. It reflects the net financial position of the government, indicating whether it is running a surplus (positive overall balance) or a deficit (negative overall balance). The overall balance takes into account all sources of revenue, including taxes, fees, and grants, as well as all types of expenditure, such as salaries, public services, and debt servicing costs. Monitoring the overall balance is crucial for assessing the government's fiscal health, its ability to meet its financial obligations, and the sustainability of its budgetary policies

str:Code.com:Name: Overall balance excluding oil revenue
str:Code.com:Description: The overall balance represents the difference between a government's total revenue and its total expenditure over a specific period, typically a fiscal year. It reflects the net financial position of the government, indicating whether it is running a surplus (positive overall balance) or a deficit (negative overall balance). The overall balance takes into account all sources of revenue, including taxes, fees, and grants, as well as all types of expenditure, such as salaries, public services, and debt servicing costs. Monitoring the overall balance is crucial for assessing the government's fiscal health, its ability to meet its financial obligations, and the sustainability of its budgetary policies. This indicator excludes government revenues and expenditures from oil production related activities.

str:Code.com:Name: Overall balance excluding privatization
str:Code.com:Description: The government's overall fiscal balance excluding revenues from the sale of government-owned assets or enterprises.

str:Code.com:Name: Overall primary balance
str:Code.com:Description: The difference between the government's total revenue and total expenditure excluding interest payments, indicating its fiscal position before debt servicing costs.

str:Code.com:Name: Other voluntary transfers: current transfers
str:Code.com:Description: Current transfers that do not fit into specific categories and are classified as miscellaneous.

str:Code.com:Name: Other voluntary transfers
str:Code.com:Description: Financial transfers that do not fit into specific categories and are classified as miscellaneous.

str:Code.com:Name: Other voluntary transfers: capital transfers
str:Code.com:Description: Capital transfers that do not fit into specific categories and are classified as miscellaneous.

str:Code.com:Name: Output for own final use
str:Code.com:Description: Goods and services produced by the government for its own use rather than for sale or distribution.

str:Code.com:Name: Output, except other nonmarket output
str:Code.com:Description: Goods and services produced by the government for sale or distribution, excluding those produced for its own use.

str:Code.com:Name: Personal income tax (PIT)
str:Code.com:Description: Personal income tax is a tax levied on the income earned by individuals. It is imposed by government authorities at the federal, state, and local levels and is typically calculated based on a progressive tax rate structure, where higher levels of income are taxed at higher rates. Personal income tax serves as a significant source of revenue for governments, funding public services, social welfare programs, infrastructure projects, and other government expenditures.

str:Code.com:Name: Policy lending
str:Code.com:Description: Loans provided by the government to support specific policy objectives, such as economic development or social welfare.

str:Code.com:Name: Payments for other non-market output
str:Code.com:Description: Payments made for goods and services produced by non-market establishments that do not operate primarily in the market.

str:Code.com:Name: Public sector net cash requirement
str:Code.com:Description: The total amount of cash needed by the public sector to meet its financial obligations.

str:Code.com:Name: Privatization proceeds
str:Code.com:Description: Revenue received from the sale of government-owned assets or enterprises.

str:Code.com:Name: Revenue decentralization
str:Code.com:Description: The process of transferring revenue-generating powers from central to local governments to enhance local financial autonomy.

str:Code.com:Name: Reporting scale
str:Code.com:Description: The scale or level at which financial data is reported, such as national, regional, or local levels.

str:Code.com:Name: Type of general sales tax
str:Code.com:Description: VAT if the country has one, empty otherwise. VAT variable also indicated the implementation date for countires that implemented in 1980 or after.

str:Code.com:Name: Structural balance
str:Code.com:Description: The government's budget balance adjusted for the economic cycle, representing the underlying fiscal position.

str:Code.com:Name: Structural primary balance
str:Code.com:Description: The primary budget balance adjusted for the economic cycle, excluding interest payments on debt.

str:Code.com:Name: Expenditure decentralization
str:Code.com:Description: The process of transferring authority and responsibility for public spending from central to local governments.

str:Code.com:Name: Social security contribution
str:Code.com:Description: Social security contributions are?compulsory payments paid to general government that confer entitlement to receive a (contingent) future social benefit.

str:Code.com:Name: Tax revenue decentralization
str:Code.com:Description: The process of transferring tax revenue-generating powers from central to local governments to enhance local financial autonomy.

str:Code.com:Name: General sales taxes collected on imports
str:Code.com:Description: Taxes collected on imported goods and services at the point of entry into the country.

str:Code.com:Name: Total changes in net worth
str:Code.com:Description: The overall change in the government's net worth over a specific period, including changes in assets and liabilities.

str:Code.com:Name: Total outlays
str:Code.com:Description: The total amount of government expenditures, including operating expenses, capital expenditures, and transfer payments.

str:Code.com:Name: Transfer dependency method 1
str:Code.com:Description: A method used to calculate the dependency of a government entity on transfer payments from other entities.

str:Code.com:Name: Transfer dependency method 2
str:Code.com:Description: An alternative method used to calculate the dependency of a government entity on transfer payments from other entities.

str:Code.com:Name: Value-added tax (VAT)
str:Code.com:Description: A Value-Added Tax (VAT) is?a consumption tax assessed on the value added in each production stage of a good or service. Every business along the value chain receives a tax credit for the VAT already paid. The end consumer does not, making it a tax on final consumption.

str:Code.com:Name: Vertical fiscal imbalance
str:Code.com:Description: A situation where there is a mismatch between the revenue-raising powers and expenditure responsibilities of different levels of government, often requiring transfers from higher to lower levels of government to address the imbalance.

str:Code.com:Name: Fiscal revenue
str:Code.com:Description: The total amount of funds collected by a government from various sources, including taxes, fees, charges, grants, and other income.

str:Code.com:Name: Employers' social contributions: actual
str:Code.com:Description: This refers to the mandatory payments made by employers to social security programs and other social insurance schemes on behalf of their employees.

str:Code.com:Name: Employers' social contributions: imputed
str:Code.com:Description: In some cases, governments provide social benefits directly to employees or self-employed individuals without requiring employers to make explicit contributions. In these situations, an imputed value is assigned to represent the cost of these benefits if they were financed through employer contributions.

str:Code.com:Name: Other revenue: adjustment
str:Code.com:Description: This is a broad category that encompasses various adjustments and miscellaneous sources of government revenue that don't fit neatly into other classifications.

str:Code.com:Name: Taxes on immovable property
str:Code.com:Description: These are taxes levied on real estate or landed property.

str:Code.com:Name: Net lending (+) / net borrowing (-) excluding grants
str:Code.com:Description: This indicator measures the difference between a government's total revenue and its total expenditure, excluding grants from other governments or international organizations.

str:Code.com:Name: Overall fiscal balance, excluding grants
str:Code.com:Description: The difference between total revenue, excluding grants, and total expenditure over a specific period. This balance indicates the overall fiscal position of the government, excluding the impact of grants.

str:Code.com:Name: Cyclically adjusted balance (with exception for countries with adjustments beyond the cycle)
str:Code.com:Description: This is a measure of a government's budget balance that attempts to isolate the effects of the business cycle. It estimates what the budget balance would be if the economy were operating at its potential output (i.e., no inflationary or recessionary gaps).

str:Code.com:Name: Cyclically adjusted primary balance (with exception for countries with adjustments beyond the cycle)
str:Code.com:Description: This is similar to the cyclically adjusted balance but excludes net interest payments on government debt. The primary balance focuses on government revenue and expenditure related to its core operations, excluding the cost of servicing past debt.

str:Code.com:Name: Social security contributions, unallocable contributions
str:Code.com:Description: This refers to social security contributions that cannot be directly assigned or allocated to specific individuals or programs.

str:Code.com:Name: Oil revenue
str:Code.com:Description: The income a country generates from the extraction, production, and sale of crude oil and related petroleum products. This can include revenue from exports, taxes on oil companies, and royalties.

str:Code.com:Name: Revenue, including grants
str:Code.com:Description: It refers to the total income a government receives from all sources, including both its own revenue-generating activities and grants from external sources.

str:Code.com:Name: Overall primary balance excluding grants
str:Code.com:Description: This measures the difference between a government's primary revenue (excluding grants) and its primary expenditure.

str:Code.com:Name: Overall primary balance, excluding grants, excluding oil
str:Code.com:Description: This is a more specific measure that further refines the primary balance by removing both oil-related revenues and oil-related expenditures.

str:Code.com:Name: Fiscal balance calculated (oil revenue minus non-oil current account balance), central government
str:Code.com:Description: This is a specific calculation of the central government's fiscal balance that takes into account oil revenue and the non-oil current account balance.

str:Code.com:Name: Overall fiscal balance, excluding oil-related revenues
str:Code.com:Description: The difference between a government's total revenue (excluding oil revenue) and its total expenditure.

str:Code.com:Name: Operating expenditure
str:Code.com:Description: Recurring expenses incurred by the government for its day-to-day operations. This includes salaries, wages, pensions, subsidies, and other administrative costs.

str:Code.com:Name: Capital expenditures, oil related
str:Code.com:Description: Investments in long-term assets related to the oil sector, such as infrastructure for oil extraction, pipelines, and refineries.

str:Code.com:Name: Subsidies and transfers
str:Code.com:Description: Government payments to individuals or businesses for various purposes, such as social welfare programs, agricultural support, or industry subsidies.

str:Code.com:Name: Overall primary balance, excluding oil
str:Code.com:Description: Similar to the overall primary balance but excludes both oil revenue and oil-related expenditures.

str:Code.com:Name: Oil revenue from exports
str:Code.com:Description: The portion of oil revenue specifically generated from exporting crude oil and petroleum products to other countries.

str:Code.com:Name: Fiscal revenue, oil related
str:Code.com:Description: All government revenue directly related to the oil sector, including taxes, royalties, and other oil-related income.

str:Code.com:Name: National defense expenditures
str:Code.com:Description: Government spending on the military, including salaries, equipment, and operations.

str:Code.com:Name: Gross financing sources
str:Code.com:Description: This is a broad term referring to all the ways a government obtains funds to finance its activities. It includes revenue, borrowing, and other inflows.

str:Code.com:Name: Gross financing sources (general government)
str:Code.com:Description: This is more specific, focusing on the financing sources of the "general government" sector. The general government usually includes the central government, state and local governments, and social security funds. It excludes public corporations and other government-controlled entities.

str:Code.com:Name: Holding gains and losses
str:Code.com:Description: These are changes in the value of a government's assets or liabilities that are not due to transactions.

str:Code.com:Name: Expense, consumption of fixed capital
str:Code.com:Description: This refers to the decline in the value of fixed assets (like buildings, machinery, and infrastructure) over time due to wear and tear, obsolescence, or age. It's essentially the depreciation of these assets.

str:Code.com:Name: Net/Gross investment in nonfinancial assets
str:Code.com:Description: This refers to the acquisition of new nonfinancial assets (like buildings, roads, and equipment) by the government, minus the disposal of existing assets.

str:Code.com:Name: Environmental taxes
str:Code.com:Description: A tax whose tax base is a physical unit (or a proxy of it) of something that has a proven, specific, negative impact on the environment. These are subdivided into four types of taxes: taxes on energy (including fuel for transport); taxes on transport (excluding fuel for transport), taxes on pollution and taxes on resources.

str:Code.com:Name: Environmental taxes, Taxes on energy (including fuel for transport)
str:Code.com:Description: A tax whose tax base is a physical unit (or a proxy of it) of something that has a proven, specific, negative impact on the environment. These are subdivided into four types of taxes: taxes on energy (including fuel for transport); taxes on transport (excluding fuel for transport), taxes on pollution and taxes on resources.

str:Code.com:Name: Environmental taxes, Taxes on pollution
str:Code.com:Description: A tax whose tax base is a physical unit (or a proxy of it) of something that has a proven, specific, negative impact on the environment. These are subdivided into four types of taxes: taxes on energy (including fuel for transport); taxes on transport (excluding fuel for transport), taxes on pollution and taxes on resources.

str:Code.com:Name: Environmental taxes, Taxes on resources
str:Code.com:Description: A tax whose tax base is a physical unit (or a proxy of it) of something that has a proven, specific, negative impact on the environment. These are subdivided into four types of taxes: taxes on energy (including fuel for transport); taxes on transport (excluding fuel for transport), taxes on pollution and taxes on resources.

str:Code.com:Name: Environmental taxes, Taxes on transport (excluding fuel for transport)
str:Code.com:Description: A tax whose tax base is a physical unit (or a proxy of it) of something that has a proven, specific, negative impact on the environment. These are subdivided into four types of taxes: taxes on energy (including fuel for transport); taxes on transport (excluding fuel for transport), taxes on pollution and taxes on resources.

str:Code.com:Name: Revenue and grants
str:Code.com:Description: The sum of total revenue received by the government and grants receivable from various entities, including both current and capital grants.

str:Code.com:Name: Total revenue and grants (G)
str:Code.com:Description: A specific category or sub-component of total revenue and grants, potentially related to a particular type of grant, a specific source of funding, or a defined geographic area.

str:Code.com:Name: Total government expenditure (XP)
str:Code.com:Description: This refers to all spending by the government on goods, services, and transfer payments (like social benefits).

str:Code.com:Name: Net lending (+) / net borrowing (-) (XC)
str:Code.com:Description: This is a core measure of the fiscal balance. It shows the difference between a government's total revenue and its total expenditure.

str:Code.com:Name: Total expenditure including net lending / net borrowing
str:Code.com:Description: The total outflow of resources by the government over a specific period, including spending on goods and services, transfers, and net lending or net borrowing activities. Net lending refers to loans provided by the government, while net borrowing represents loans received.

str:Code.com:Name: Gross debt (XWDG)
str:Code.com:Description: This is the total amount of outstanding debt owed by the government.

str:Code.com:Name: Net debt (XWDN)
str:Code.com:Description: This is a more refined measure of government debt. It subtracts any financial assets held by the government from its gross debt.

str:Code.com:Name: Overall primary balance excluding grants (NLBXG)
str:Code.com:Description: The primary balance of the government (revenue minus expenditure, excluding interest payments), further excluding grants received.

str:Code.com:Name: Primary fiscal balance, excluding oil related transactions
str:Code.com:Description: The primary balance of the government (revenue minus expenditure, excluding interest payments), excluding revenue and expenditure directly related to oil extraction, production, and sales.

str:Code.com:Name: Fiscal impulse (EUR)
str:Code.com:Description: A measure of the change in the fiscal stance of the government, indicating the impact of fiscal policy on economic activity.

str:Code.com:Name: Transfers payable
str:Code.com:Description: Transfers, payable including pensions, social security, unemployment insurances, other transfers n.e.c.

str:Code.com:Name: Financing of additional infrastructure / public spending if not already incorporated in cyclically adjusted primary balance
str:Code.com:Description: Financing of additional infrastructure/public spending if not already incorporated in cyclically adjusted primary balance

str:Code.com:Name: Recommended cyclically adjusted primary balance
str:Code.com:Description: This is a measure used to assess a government's fiscal position

str:Code.com:Name: Capital taxes
str:Code.com:Description: These are taxes on things people own, like property (real estate), stocks, bonds, and other investments. They can also include taxes on wealth transfers, such as inheritance taxes.

str:Code.com:Name: Labor taxes
str:Code.com:Description: These are taxes on people's income from working.

str:Code.com:Name: Current and projected infrastructure / public investment
str:Code.com:Description: Current and projected infrastructure/public investment.

str:Code.com:Name: Recommended infrastructure / public investment
str:Code.com:Description: Recommended infrastructure/public investment.

str:Code.com:Name: Expenditure decentralization (ratio of own spending to general government spending)
str:Code.com:Description: A measure of expenditure decentralization, calculated as the ratio of sub-national government spending to general government spending over a specific period. This ratio indicates the distribution of spending authority between different levels of government.

str:Code.com:Name: Fiscal balance, excluding oil related transactions
str:Code.com:Description: The overall fiscal balance of the government (total revenue minus total expenditure), excluding revenue and expenditure directly related to oil extraction, production, and sales.

str:Code.com:Name: Other public financing sources, GG gross financing sources
str:Code.com:Description: Public financing sources other than traditional debt or revenue, such as asset sales, privatization proceeds, or other non-debt financing instruments, contributing to the general government's gross financing needs.

str:Code.com:Name: Domestic debt gross issuance, GG gross financing sources
str:Code.com:Description: The total value of new debt instruments issued by the government within its own country, contributing to general government gross financing sources.

str:Code.com:Name: External debt gross issuance, GG gross financing sources
str:Code.com:Description: The total value of new debt instruments issued by the government in foreign markets or to foreign lenders, contributing to general government gross financing sources.

str:Code.com:Name: External market debt issuance, GG gross financing sources
str:Code.com:Description: The total value of new debt instruments issued by the government specifically in international capital markets, a subset of external debt issuance, contributing to general government gross financing sources.

str:Code.com:Name: Domestic amortization, GG gross financing needs
str:Code.com:Description: The repayment of principal on outstanding domestic debt obligations by the government, contributing to general government gross financing needs.

str:Code.com:Name: External amortization, GG gross financing needs
str:Code.com:Description: The repayment of principal on outstanding external debt obligations by the government, contributing to general government gross financing needs.

str:Code.com:Name: Net investment in nonfinancial assets, Acquisitions (++)
str:Code.com:Description: The net investment (acquisitions minus disposals) by the government in nonfinancial assets, such as infrastructure, buildings, and equipment, indicating a higher level or intensity of acquisition activity.

str:Code.com:Name: Net investment in nonfinancial assets, Acquisitions (+)
str:Code.com:Description: The net investment (acquisitions minus disposals) by the government in nonfinancial assets, such as infrastructure, buildings, and equipment, indicating a moderate level of acquisition activity.

str:Code.com:Name: Total
str:Code.com:Description: The sum of all relevant components or categories.

str:Code.com:Name: Net acquisition of financial assets, Policy lending
str:Code.com:Description: Loans provided by the government to support specific policy objectives, such as economic development or social welfare.

str:Code.com:Name: Net acquisition of financial assets: foreign
str:Code.com:Description: The difference between the acquisition and disposal of foreign financial assets by the government, representing the net increase in financial assets.

str:Code.com:Name: Domestic excluding cash
str:Code.com:Description: Domestic excluding cash.

str:Code.com:Name: Net investement in nonfinanical assets, Consumption of fixed capital (--)
str:Code.com:Description: Net investment in nonfinancial assets, Consumption of fixed capital (--).

str:Code.com:Name: Gross debt at market value
str:Code.com:Description: Gross debt is the sum of all liabilities that are debt instruments. Debt instruments are financial instruments that require the payment of principal and/or interest at some point(s) in the future. Debt instruments consist of SDRs, currency and deposits, debt securities, loans, insurance technical reserves, pension and related entitlements, provision for calls under standardized guarantees, and other accounts receivable/payable. Debt is valued at markt value.

str:Code.com:Name: Adjustment (GFS)
str:Code.com:Description: Adjustment (GFS).

str:Code.com:Name: Stock-flow discrepancy, Change in net worth vs change in stocks
str:Code.com:Description: Stock-flow discrepancy, Change in net worth vs change in stocks.

str:Code.com:Name: Stock-flow discrepancy
str:Code.com:Description: Stock-flow discrepancy.

str:Code.com:Name: Buildings other than dwellings (acquisitions less disposals)
str:Code.com:Description: Buildings other than dwellings (acquisitions less disposals).

str:Code.com:Name: Machinery and equipment (acquisitions less disposals)
str:Code.com:Description: Machinery and equipment (acquisitions less disposals).

str:Code.com:Name: Weapons systems (acquisitions less disposals)
str:Code.com:Description: Weapons systems (acquisitions less disposals).

str:Code.com:Name: Inventories by type of inventory (acquisitions less disposals)
str:Code.com:Description: Inventories by type of inventory (acquisitions less disposals).

str:Code.com:Name: Buildings and structures
str:Code.com:Description: Buildings and structures.

str:Code.com:Name: Buildings and structures (acquisitions less disposals)
str:Code.com:Description: Buildings and structures (acquisitions less disposals).

str:Code.com:Name: Social security benefits: Unemployment benefits
str:Code.com:Description: Social security benefits: Unemployment benefits.

str:Code.com:Name: Social security benefits: Family benefits
str:Code.com:Description: Social security benefits: Family benefits.

str:Code.com:Name: Social security benefits: Care benefits
str:Code.com:Description: Social security benefits: Care benefits.

str:Code.com:Name: Social security benefits: In-work benefits
str:Code.com:Description: Social security benefits: In-work benefits.

str:Codelist.com:Name: Exchange Rate
str:Codelist.com:Description: Represents a set of codes identifying exchange rates between various currencies, including period averages, pre-Euro rates, and those involving Special Drawing Rights (SDRs).

str:Code.com:Name: European Currency Unit per domestic currency
str:Code.com:Description: Exchange rate between European Currency Unit (ECU) and a national currency.

str:Code.com:Name: Euro per domestic currency
str:Code.com:Description: Exchange rate indicating how many Euros are equivalent to one unit of domestic currency.

str:Code.com:Name: End-of-period exchange rate
str:Code.com:Description: The end-of-period exchange rate refers to the exchange rate between two currencies at the end of a specific accounting or reporting period. This is typically the last business day of the month, quarter, or year.

str:Code.com:Name: Foreign currency paid in Euro
str:Code.com:Description: Foreign exchange payment for goods or services denominated and paid in Euros.

str:Code.com:Name: Foreign currency paid in US dollar
str:Code.com:Description: Foreign exchange payment for goods or services denominated and paid in US dollars.

str:Code.com:Name: Foreign currency paid in domestic currency
str:Code.com:Description: Foreign exchange payment for goods or services denominated and paid in domestic currency.

str:Code.com:Name: Pound sterling per US dollar
str:Code.com:Description: Exchange rate indicating how many Pound sterling are equivalent to one US dollar.

str:Code.com:Name: US dollar per domestic currency
str:Code.com:Description: Exchange rate indicating how many US dollars are equivalent to one unit of domestic currency. This is the inverse of the "Domestic currency per US dollar" rate.

str:Code.com:Name: US dollar per Special drawing rights
str:Code.com:Description: Exchange rate indicating how many US dollars are equivalent to one unit of Special Drawing Right (SDR).

str:Code.com:Name: Domestic currency per European Currency Unit
str:Code.com:Description: Exchange rate indicating how many units of domestic currency are equivalent to one European Currency Unit (ECU).

str:Code.com:Name: Domestic currency per end-of-period domestic currency
str:Code.com:Description: Comparison of the domestic currency's value at the end of a specific period to its value at some other point in time. The end-of-period could be the end of a month, quarter, or year.

str:Code.com:Name: Domestic currency per Euro
str:Code.com:Description: Exchange rate indicating how many units of domestic currency are equivalent to one Euro.

str:Code.com:Name: Domestic currency per PPP US dollar
str:Code.com:Description: Purchasing power parity conversion factor showing the amount of domestic currency required to achieve the same purchasing power as one US dollar.

str:Code.com:Name: Domestic currency per international dollar in PPP terms
str:Code.com:Description: Purchasing power parity conversion factor showing the amount of domestic currency required to achieve the same purchasing power as one international dollar.

str:Code.com:Name: Domestic currency per Special drawing rights
str:Code.com:Description: Exchange rate indicating how many units of domestic currency are equivalent to one unit of Special Drawing Right (SDR).

str:Code.com:Name: Domestic currency per US dollar
str:Code.com:Description: Exchange rate indicating how many units of domestic currency are equivalent to one US dollar.

str:Code.com:Name: Special drawing rights per US dollar
str:Code.com:Description: Exchange rate indicating how many units of Special Drawing Rights (SDRs) are equivalent to one US dollar.

str:Code.com:Name: Special drawing rights per domestic currency
str:Code.com:Description: Exchange rate indicating how many Special Drawing Rights (SDRs) are equivalent to one unit of domestic currency.

str:Code.com:Name: Domestic currency per Special drawing rights
str:Code.com:Description: Exchange rate indicating how many units domestic currency are equivalent to one unit of Special Drawing Right (SDR).

str:Code.com:Name: Domestic currency per US Dollar pre-EURO
str:Code.com:Description: Exchange rate indicating how many units domestic currency are equivalent to one US dollar, pre-Euro.

str:Code.com:Name: US Dollars per ounce of gold
str:Code.com:Description: US Dollars per ounce of gold.

str:Code.com:Name: Special drawing rights per ounce of gold
str:Code.com:Description: Special drawing rights per ounce of gold.

str:Code.com:Name: Real Domestic Currency per US Dollar
str:Code.com:Description: Real Domestic Currency per US Dollar.

str:Code.com:Name: Domestic currency per Pound sterling
str:Code.com:Description: Exchange rate indicating how many units domestic currency are equivalent to one Pound sterling.

str:Code.com:Name: Domestic currency per French franc
str:Code.com:Description: Exchange rate indicating how many units domestic currency are equivalent to one French franc.

str:Code.com:Name: Domestic currency per Deutsche mark
str:Code.com:Description: Exchange rate indicating how many units domestic currency are equivalent to one Deutsche mark.

str:Code.com:Name: Domestic currency per Norwegian krone
str:Code.com:Description: Exchange rate indicating how many units domestic currency are equivalent to one Norwegian krone.

str:Code.com:Name: Domestic currency per Swedish krona
str:Code.com:Description: Exchange rate indicating how many units domestic currency are equivalent to one Swedish krona.

str:Code.com:Name: Domestic currency per Canadian dollar
str:Code.com:Description: Exchange rate indicating how many units domestic currency are equivalent to one Canadian dollar.

str:Code.com:Name: Domestic currency per Japanese yen
str:Code.com:Description: Exchange rate indicating how many units domestic currency are equivalent to one Japanese yen.

str:Code.com:Name: Domestic currency per Australian dollar
str:Code.com:Description: Exchange rate indicating how many units domestic currency are equivalent to one Australian dollar.

str:Code.com:Name: Domestic currency per New Zealand dollar
str:Code.com:Description: Exchange rate indicating how many units domestic currency are equivalent to one New Zealand dollar.

str:Code.com:Name: Domestic currency per Sri Lankan rupee
str:Code.com:Description: Exchange rate indicating how many units domestic currency are equivalent to one Sri Lankan rupee.

str:Code.com:Name: Domestic currency per Indian rupee
str:Code.com:Description: Exchange rate indicating how many units domestic currency are equivalent to one Indian rupee.

str:Code.com:Name: Domestic currency per Malaysian ringgit
str:Code.com:Description: Exchange rate indicating how many units domestic currency are equivalent to one Malaysian ringgit.

str:Code.com:Name: Domestic currency per Singapore dollar
str:Code.com:Description: Exchange rate indicating how many units domestic currency are equivalent to one Singapore dollar.

str:Code.com:Name: Domestic currency per Chinese yuan
str:Code.com:Description: Exchange rate indicating how many units domestic currency are equivalent to one Chinese yuan.

str:Codelist.com:Name: Access and Sharing Level
str:Codelist.com:Description: This codelist defines the allowable levels of access and sharing for a particular data resource, specifying who can view, use, or distribute the data and under what conditions.

str:Code.com:Name: موظفو صندوق النقد الدولي المعتمدون
Уполномоченный персонал МВФ
Funcionários autorizados do FMI
許可されたIMF職員
Authorized IMF staff
Personnel autorisé du FMI
Personal autorizado del FMI
授权的国际货币基金组织工作人员
str:Code.com:Description: شارك هذه البيانات فقط مع موظفي صندوق النقد الدولي المعتمدين الذين لديهم حاجة مشروعة لها. بالنسبة للبيانات الحساسة، قد يلزم الحصول على إذن كتابي من مُنشئ البيانات أو موافقة المدير.

Предоставляйте эти данные только уполномоченным сотрудникам МВФ, которым они необходимы по долгу службы. Для конфиденциальных данных может потребоваться письменное разрешение создателя данных или утверждение руководителя.

Compartilhe estes dados apenas com funcionários autorizados do FMI que tenham uma necessidade legítima para tal. Para dados sensíveis, pode ser necessária uma permissão por escrito do criador dos dados ou a aprovação do gestor.
このデータは、正当な必要性を持つ許可されたIMF職員とのみ共有してください。機密データの場合、データ作成者からの書面による許可または管理者の承認が必要になる場合があります。
Share this data only with authorized IMF staff who have a need to know. For sensitive data, written permission from the data creator or manager approval may be required.

Partagez ces données uniquement avec les membres du personnel autorisés du FMI qui en ont un besoin légitime. Pour les données sensibles, une autorisation écrite du créateur des données ou l'approbation du responsable peut être requise.

Comparta estos datos solo con el personal autorizado del FMI que tenga una necesidad legítima de ellos. Para datos confidenciales, puede requerirse permiso por escrito del creador de los datos o la aprobación del gerente.
仅与有合法需求的授权国际货币基金组织工作人员共享此数据。 对于敏感数据，可能需要数据创建者或经理的书面许可。
